<script type="text/javascript">
var maindomain_lang = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
var main_site_url = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
var ajax_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-admin/admin-ajax.php';	
function getParameterByName(name, url) {
		if(url == null){
		  url = window.location.href;
		}
	    name = name.replace(/[\[\]]/g, '\\$&');
	    var regex = new RegExp('[?&]' + name + '(=([^&#]*)|&|#|$)'),
		results = regex.exec(url);
	    if (!results) return null;
	    if (!results[2]) return '';
	    return decodeURIComponent(results[2].replace(/\+/g, ' '));
	}
	var container_q = getParameterByName('container');
	var d_lang = 'en';	
</script>

<meta property="og:readtime" content="10"/>
<meta property="og:views" content="2,169"/>
<meta property="og:date_created" content="January 20, 2026 at 9:23 AM"/>
<meta property="og:post_length" content="2729"/>

<meta property="og:category_link" content="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/category/threat-research/"/>
<meta property="og:author" content="Golan Myers"/>
<meta property="og:authorlink" content="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/author/"/>
<meta property="og:author_image_link" content="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/11/unit-news-meta.svg"/>
<meta name="post_tags" content="Microsoft Azure,networking"/>
<script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"https:\/\/schema.org","@type":"BlogPosting","headline":"DNS OverDoS: Are Private Endpoints Too Private?","name":"DNS OverDoS: Are Private Endpoints Too Private?","description":"We've identified an aspect of Azure\u2019s Private Endpoint architecture that could expose Azure resources to denial of service (DoS) attacks.","url":"https:\/\/unit42.paloaltonetworks.com\/dos-attacks-and-azure-private-endpoint\/","mainEntityOfPage":"https:\/\/unit42.paloaltonetworks.com\/dos-attacks-and-azure-private-endpoint\/","datePublished":"January 20, 2026","articleBody":"Executive Summary\r\nWe discovered an aspect of Azure\u2019s Private Endpoint architecture that could expose Azure resources to denial of service (DoS) attacks. In this article, we explore how both intentional and inadvertent acts could result in limited access to Azure resources through the Azure Private Link mechanism. We uncovered this issue while investigating irregular behavior in Azure test environments.\r\n\r\nThe risk is present in three scenarios:\r\n\r\n \tAccidental - internal: A network administrator deploys Private Endpoints to improve network security within an Azure environment.\r\n \tAccidental - vendor: A third-party vendor deploys Private Endpoints as part of its solution, for example to enable resource scanning by a security product.\r\n \tMalicious - attacker: A threat actor who gained access to an Azure environment intentionally deploys Private Endpoints as part of a DoS attack.\r\n\r\nOur research indicates that over 5% of Azure storage accounts currently operate with configurations that are subject to this DoS issue. In most environments, at least one resource in each of the following services is susceptible:\r\n\r\n \tKey Vault\r\n \tCosmosDB\r\n \tAzure Container Registry (ACR)\r\n \tFunction Apps\r\n \tOpenAI accounts\r\n\r\nThis issue has the potential to affect organizations in multiple ways. For example, denying service to storage accounts could cause Azure Functions within FunctionApps and subsequent updates to these apps to fail. In another scenario, the risk could lead to DoS to Key Vaults, resulting in a ripple effect on processes that depend on secrets within the vault.\r\n\r\nMicrosoft provides fallback to internet advice that partially addresses this and other known issues associated with Private Endpoints.\r\n\r\nWe discuss these issues, provide potential solutions and suggest ways that defenders can scan environments for resources that are susceptible to DoS attacks.\r\n\r\nPalo Alto Networks customers are better protected from the threats discussed in this article through the following products:\r\n\r\n \tCortex Cloud\r\n\r\nUnit 42 Cloud Security Assessment is a strategic evaluation service that reviews your organization's cloud infrastructure to identify misconfigurations and security gaps, enabling teams to strengthen their posture against cloud-based threats.\r\n\r\nIf you think you might have been compromised or have an urgent matter, contact the Unit 42 Incident Response team.\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nRelated Unit 42 Topics\r\nMicrosoft Azure, Cloud Research\r\n\r\n\r\n\r\nAzure Private Link Key Components, Concepts and Flows\r\nAs part of Azure\u2019s networking offering, Microsoft created Azure Private Link. This mechanism offers a private, secure way to connect to supported Azure resources and Azure-hosted custom services using Azure\u2019s backbone network.\r\nDefinitions\r\nTo understand the solution and the secure connection process of resources using it, let\u2019s first define the key components and concepts.\r\n\r\n \tService: Destination to which a connection is made\r\n \tPrivate Link: Azure implementation that allows and handles the connections\r\n \tPrivate Endpoint: A network interface within a customer\u2019s virtual network that allows connectivity to the service\r\n \tPrivate DNS zone: The default Domain Name System (DNS) service that is used with Private Endpoints\r\n \tVirtual network link: The link created by default between a Private DNS zone and a virtual network\r\n \tDNS A record: A DNS record that maps a domain or hostname to an IP address\r\n \tNetwork ACL: Network access control lists (ACLs) define rules that allow or restrict traffic to a service, separate from the Private Link solution\r\n\r\nAzure resources expose public endpoints by default. These endpoints resolve through standard DNS infrastructure \u2014 either public Azure DNS, or customer-managed DNS resolvers. When a client queries a service name such as mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows[.]net, the DNS resolver returns a public IP address owned by Microsoft. The IP address allows connectivity over the public internet or Azure service endpoints, depending on the network access controls that are applied.\r\n\r\nWhen Azure Private Link is introduced, DNS resolution behavior changes. A Private DNS zone \u2014 for example, privatelink.blob.core.windows[.]net \u2014 can be linked to one or more virtual networks. If a virtual network has a link to a Private DNS zone for a given service type, Azure\u2019s DNS resolution logic prioritizes that zone when resolving matching service names. If a matching record exists, the name resolves to the private IP address of the Private Endpoint, instead of the public endpoint.\r\n\r\nOrganizations commonly deploy one of the following architectures:\r\n\r\n \tPublic-only architecture: Resources are accessed using their public endpoints and standard DNS resolution. Network ACLs, firewalls or service endpoints restrict access.\r\n \tPrivate-only architecture: All access to the resource occurs through Private Endpoints. Public network access is disabled, and DNS resolution is fully controlled through Private DNS zones.\r\n \tHybrid architecture (public and private): Some virtual networks or workloads access the resource through Private Endpoints, while others continue using the public endpoint. This model is frequently used during migrations, phased rollouts, third-party integrations, or shared service environments.\r\n\r\nWe use the example of storage accounts to demonstrate how Private Endpoints are used in Azure networking. However, the same concepts apply to any Private Link-supported service. By default, when a network administrator or a user with appropriate Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) permissions creates a Private Endpoint linked to a resource, a Private DNS zone is created with a virtual network link to the same virtual network as the Private Endpoint.\r\n\r\nThe DNS zone\u2019s name has a predetermined structure that is based on the Private Endpoint\u2019s destination service (e.g., privatelink.blob.core.windows[.]net for blob storage). Additionally, an A record is created in the DNS zone, linking the name of the destination resource and the IP address of the Private Endpoint.\r\n\r\nThere are two ways to connect between a virtual machine VM and a storage account with a Network ACL:\r\n\r\n \tWithout a Private Endpoint (using public or service endpoints)\r\n \tWith a Private Endpoint\r\n\r\nConnection Flows\r\nFigure 1 shows how a connection is made to a resource that does not use the Private Link solution.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_170708\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"761\"] Figure 1. Connection flow without the Private Link solution.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nThe flow in this case is as follows:\r\n\r\n \tWhen trying to access the storage account, the VM attempts to resolve the account\u2019s name to an IP address. This usually occurs through an external DNS resolver, and the traffic traverses the public internet.\r\n \tIf the resolver has a record for the storage account, it returns the corresponding IP address.\r\n \tOnce the address is obtained, the VM tries to connect to the storage account.\r\n \tThe storage account then evaluates the virtual machine\u2019s IP address against its Network ACL. If the connection is allowed, the storage account responds with the requested information.\r\n\r\nFigure 2 shows how the same connection is made when a Private Endpoint is used.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_170719\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"808\"] Figure 2. Connection flow with the Private Link solution.[\/caption]\r\n\r\n \tInitially, the VM attempts to resolve the account\u2019s name to an IP address. If a virtual network link exists in the virtual network (VNET) to a Private DNS zone that points to a Private Link service, Azure\u2019s Private Link mechanism forces resolution using the Private DNS zone. This occurs when the connection is to a Private Link service of the same type (e.g., blob storage).\r\n \tWhen the DNS resolver identifies a matching A record, it provides the VM with the corresponding IP address.\r\n \tThe VM then connects to the IP address, which belongs to the Private Endpoint.\r\n \tThe Private Endpoint acts as a network interface for the storage account, evaluating the traffic and then forwarding it to the storage account.\r\n \tThe storage account evaluates the request and supplies a response through the Private Link solution.\r\n \tThe response is presented to the VM, which essentially accesses the storage account using Azure\u2019s backbone network.\r\n\r\nPotential Private Link DNS Dangers\r\nAs outlined above, interactions with a Private Link service from a virtual network configured with a Private DNS zone for that service type are forced to resolve through the Private DNS zone.\r\n\r\nConsider an environment where VM1 in VNET1 successfully accesses a storage account using its public endpoint. DNS resolution occurs through the default public DNS infrastructure, and access is permitted by the storage account\u2019s Network ACLs. At this stage, the workload is operating normally.\r\n\r\nLater, a Private Endpoint is created for the storage account in VNET2 \u2014 either intentionally, accidentally, or by a third-party deployment. As part of this process, a Private DNS zone for blob storage (privatelink.blob.core.windows[.]net) is linked to VNET1 or shared across virtual networks.\r\n\r\nOnce this DNS zone is linked, Azure\u2019s DNS resolution logic forces all blob storage name resolution in VNET1 through the Private DNS zone. However, because no \u201cA\u201d record exists in the zone for the storage account within the context of VNET1, DNS resolution fails. VM1 can no longer resolve the storage account hostname and is unable to connect \u2014 even though the public endpoint remains accessible and unchanged.\r\n\r\nThis configuration change effectively creates a denial-of-service condition for workloads in VNET1 that previously functioned correctly. The outage is triggered solely by a DNS resolution side effect introduced by the Private Link configuration, without any change to the target resource itself.\r\n\r\nFigure 3 demonstrates this example.\r\n\r\n[caption id=\"attachment_170730\" align=\"alignnone\" width=\"939\"] Figure 3. Potential issue caused by using the Private Link solution.[\/caption]\r\n\r\nThe figure above shows that VM1 attempts to connect to the storage account. This storage account has a Private Endpoint in VNET2 and does not have a Private Endpoint in VNET1. Hypothetically, VM1 could try to connect using the storage account\u2019s public endpoint. This would work if the VM\u2019s virtual network did not have a Private DNS zone resolving to the same resource type. In this case, however, the Private DNS zone and the service are Private Link registered, so Private Link forces resolution through the Private DNS zone.\r\n\r\nThe process shown in Figure 3 occurs as follows:\r\n\r\n \tPrivate Link recognizes the blob storage Private DNS zone in VNET1 and identifies that the storage account is Private Link registered. Private Link forces DNS resolution through the Private DNS zone.\r\n \tThe configuration in Figure 3 shows that no record was created for the storage account in the Private DNS zone linked to VNET1. As such, the virtual machine cannot resolve the service.\r\n \tDue to the DNS resolution issue in step 2, the subsequent steps \u2013 in which VM1 sends a request to the storage account and receives a response from it \u2013 do not occur.\r\n\r\nThis scenario causes a partial denial of service: any resource in VNET1 that tries to access the storage account will not be able to.\r\n\r\nThis risk can also be present when using local DNS resolvers, depending on the configurations and the records added. Additionally, although our example relates specifically to blob storage, any service that supports Azure Private Link is potentially at risk.\r\nMitigations and Recommendations\r\nAzure Private Link was originally intended as a binary solution, to be either fully enabled, or disabled. When the service is implemented as intended, users can only access resources that use Private Link through those resources' Private Endpoints. This approach eliminates the need to use a combination of private, public and service endpoints. However, as over 5% of Azure storage accounts are configured in a way that is subject to this issue, we recognize the need to provide solutions that address the risks introduced by these implementations.\r\nMitigations\r\nMicrosoft acknowledges that the binary nature of the solution is a known issue and mentions it in their documentation. Microsoft also provides a partial solution: enabling a fallback to internet option when creating a virtual network link. Using this fallback solution, when the DNS Resolver cannot find a record that matches the requested service, it falls back to the public internet. While this solution solves the issue, it does not necessarily match the main concept of Azure Private Link, which is to traverse Azure\u2019s backbone network rather than the public internet.\r\n\r\nA second, partial solution is to manually add a record for the affected resource in the necessary Private DNS zone. This option is less appropriate for large production environments, as it creates additional operational overhead.\r\n\r\nAlthough neither fallback nor manually adding a record is a definitive solution, combining the above approaches with comprehensive discovery will help with mapping, finding and remediating affected configurations.\r\nComprehensive Discovery and Scanning\r\nThere are two ways to scan and identify resources that are potentially at risk:\r\n\r\n \tScanning for resources with each service individually\r\n \tUsing Azure Resource Graph Explorer\r\n\r\nThe second scanning method is more efficient and can easily be modified to match most resource types. We created a graph query that retrieves a list of all the virtual networks in the environment that are linked to a blob storage Private DNS zone (privatelink.blob.core.windows[.]net). These virtual networks are forced to resolve blob storage resources through their Private Endpoints when communicating with Private Link registered resources.\r\nresources\r\n| where type == \"microsoft.network\/privatednszones\/virtualnetworklinks\"\r\n| extend \r\n    zone = tostring(split(id, \"\/virtualNetworkLinks\")[0]),\r\n    vnetId = tostring(properties.virtualNetwork.id)\r\n| join kind=inner (\r\n    resources\r\n    | where type == \"microsoft.network\/privatednszones\"\r\n    | where name == \"privatelink.blob.core.windows.net\"\r\n    | project zoneId = id\r\n) on $left.zone == $right.zoneId\r\n| project vnetId\r\n\r\nIn addition, it is important to identify which virtual networks interact with blob storage resources that do not have Private Endpoint connections. We created the following query for this purpose. This query retrieves storage accounts that allow access to their public endpoint and do not have a Private Endpoint connection.\r\nResources\r\n| where type == \"microsoft.storage\/storageaccounts\"\r\n| extend publicNetworkAccess = properties.publicNetworkAccess\r\n| extend defaultAction = properties.networkAcls.defaultAction\r\n| extend vnetRules = properties.networkAcls.virtualNetworkRules\r\n| extend ipRules = properties.networkAcls.ipRules\r\n| extend privateEndpoints = properties.privateEndpointConnections\r\n| where publicNetworkAccess == \"Enabled\"\r\n| where defaultAction == \"Deny\"\r\n| where (isnull(privateEndpoints) or array_length(privateEndpoints) == 0)\r\n| extend allowedVnets = iif(isnull(vnetRules), 0, array_length(vnetRules))\r\n| extend allowedIps = iif(isnull(ipRules), 0, array_length(ipRules))\r\n| where allowedVnets &gt; 0 or allowedIps &gt; 0\r\n| project id, name, vnetRules, ipRules\r\nThis query identifies allowed virtual networks and also retrieves resources that allow specific IP addresses. This is useful when there is limited visibility into the DNS configurations for virtual networks, making it difficult to determine whether or not the configurations are affected.\r\n\r\nDefenders should be aware that even resources that do not have Network ACLs could still be at risk. However, there is no way to use the configurations to determine which virtual networks, if any, attempt to communicate with such resources. To address this risk, use network logs to identify connections made from Azure network ranges to susceptible resources.\r\n\r\nDefenders can change and match the resource and the Private DNS zone details in the above Resources query to detect Private Link-supported resources that are at risk.\r\nConclusion\r\nFully understanding the limitations of Azure Private Link is vital to securing networks that rely on it. With certain configurations of components and architecture, the binary nature of Azure Private Link can lead to connectivity loss and in some cases, could enable DoS attacks.\r\n\r\nTwo of the possible solutions to secure Private Endpoints against this risk include:\r\n\r\n \tEnabling fallback to public DNS resolution\r\n \tManually adding DNS records for affected resources\r\n\r\nDefenders can increase the effectiveness of these solutions by querying resources, conducting comprehensive network scanning to identify resources at risk, and implementing the necessary protections.\r\nPalo Alto Networks Protection and Mitigation\r\nPalo Alto Networks customers are better protected from the threats discussed in this article through the following products:\r\n\r\n \tCortex Cloud customers are better protected from the misuse of the Azure Private Endpoints discussed within this article through the proper placement of Cortex Cloud XDR endpoint agent and serverless agents within a cloud environment. Cortex Cloud is designed to protect a cloud\u2019s posture and runtime operations against these types of threats. It helps detect and prevent the malicious operations or configuration alterations or exploitation discussed within this article.\r\n\r\nUnit 42 Cloud Security Assessment is a strategic evaluation service that reviews your organization's cloud infrastructure to identify misconfigurations and security gaps, enabling teams to strengthen their posture against cloud-based threats.\r\n\r\nIf you think you might have been compromised or have an urgent matter, get in touch with the Unit 42 Incident Response team or call:\r\n\r\n \tNorth America: Toll Free: +1 (866) 486-4842 (866.4.UNIT42)\r\n \tUK: +44.20.3743.3660\r\n \tEurope and Middle East: +31.20.299.3130\r\n \tAsia: +65.6983.8730\r\n \tJapan: +81.50.1790.0200\r\n \tAustralia: +61.2.4062.7950\r\n \tIndia: 000 800 050 45107\r\n\r\nPalo Alto Networks has shared these findings with our fellow Cyber Threat Alliance (CTA) members. CTA members use this intelligence to rapidly deploy protections to their customers and to systematically disrupt malicious cyber actors. Learn more about the Cyber Threat Alliance.\r\nAdditional Resources\r\n\r\n \tAzure Private Endpoint private DNS zone values \u2013 Microsoft documentation\r\n \tAzure Private Link availability \u2013 Microsoft documentation\r\n \tAzure Resource Graph Explorer \u2013 Microsoft documentation\r\n \t\u200b\u200bStorage access constraints for clients in virtual networks with private endpoints \u2013 Microsoft documentation\r\n","publisher":{"@type":"Organization","@id":"#panworg"},"image":{"@type":"ImageObject","url":"https:\/\/unit42.paloaltonetworks.com\/wp-content\/uploads\/2025\/06\/02_DNS_Overview_1920x900-300x300.jpg","width":300,"height":300},"speakable":{"@type":"SpeakableSpecification","xPath":["\/html\/head\/title","\/html\/head\/meta[@name='description']\/@content"]},"author":[{"@type":"Person","name":"Golan Myers"}]}</script><style id='wp-img-auto-sizes-contain-inline-css'>
img:is([sizes=auto i],[sizes^="auto," i]){contain-intrinsic-size:3000px 1500px}

</style>

    <script type="text/javascript">
    ;(function(win, doc, style, timeout) {
    var STYLE_ID = 'at-body-style';
    function getParent() {
    return doc.getElementsByTagName('head')[0];
    }
    function addStyle(parent, id, def) {
        if (!parent) {
        return;
        }
        var style = doc.createElement('style');
        style.id = id;
        style.innerHTML = def;
        parent.appendChild(style);
    }
    function removeStyle(parent, id) {
        if (!parent) {
            return;
        }
        var style = doc.getElementById(id);
        if (!style) {
            return;
        }
        parent.removeChild(style);
    }
    addStyle(getParent(), STYLE_ID, style);
    setTimeout(function() {
    removeStyle(getParent(), STYLE_ID);
    }, timeout);
    }(window, document, "body {visibility:hidden !important}", 3000));
    </script>

<script type="text/javascript">
var isIE11 = !!navigator.userAgent.match(/Trident.*rv\:11\./);
if(isIE11){
    var polyfill = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/scripts/polyfill.min.js';
    document.write('<script type="text/javascript" src="'+polyfill+'">\x3C/script>');
}
    /**
 * String.prototype.replaceAll() polyfill
 * https://gomakethings.com/how-to-replace-a-section-of-a-string-with-another-one-with-vanilla-js/
 * @author Chris Ferdinandi
 * @license MIT
 */
if (!String.prototype.replaceAll) {
	String.prototype.replaceAll = function(str, newStr){
		// If a regex pattern
		if (Object.prototype.toString.call(str).toLowerCase() === '[object regexp]') {
			return this.replace(str, newStr);
		}
		// If a string
		return this.replace(new RegExp(str, 'g'), newStr);
	};
}

    /*! lozad.js - v1.16.0 - 2020-09-06 */
!function(t,e){"object"==typeof exports&&"undefined"!=typeof module?module.exports=e():"function"==typeof define&&define.amd?define(e):t.lozad=e()}(this,function(){"use strict";
/**
   * Detect IE browser
   * @const {boolean}
   * @private
   */var g="undefined"!=typeof document&&document.documentMode,f={rootMargin:"0px",threshold:0,load:function(t){if("picture"===t.nodeName.toLowerCase()){var e=t.querySelector("img"),r=!1;null===e&&(e=document.createElement("img"),r=!0),g&&t.getAttribute("data-iesrc")&&(e.src=t.getAttribute("data-iesrc")),t.getAttribute("data-alt")&&(e.alt=t.getAttribute("data-alt")),r&&t.append(e)}if("video"===t.nodeName.toLowerCase()&&!t.getAttribute("data-src")&&t.children){for(var a=t.children,o=void 0,i=0;i<=a.length-1;i++)(o=a[i].getAttribute("data-src"))&&(a[i].src=o);t.load()}t.getAttribute("data-poster")&&(t.poster=t.getAttribute("data-poster")),t.getAttribute("data-src")&&(t.src=t.getAttribute("data-src")),t.getAttribute("data-srcset")&&t.setAttribute("srcset",t.getAttribute("data-srcset"));var n=",";if(t.getAttribute("data-background-delimiter")&&(n=t.getAttribute("data-background-delimiter")),t.getAttribute("data-background-image"))t.style.backgroundImage="url('"+t.getAttribute("data-background-image").split(n).join("'),url('")+"')";else if(t.getAttribute("data-background-image-set")){var d=t.getAttribute("data-background-image-set").split(n),u=d[0].substr(0,d[0].indexOf(" "))||d[0];// Substring before ... 1x

</script>

  </div>
</header>

<nav id="navigation" class="site-nav collapse d-sm-block pb-20 mt-sm-10"  style="display: none!important;">
  <div class="container px-sm-30">
    <ul id="menu-primary-navigation" class="main-menu d-sm-flex font-weight-medium"><li id="menu-item-97290" class="menu-item menu-item-type-post_type menu-item-object-page menu-item-97290"><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tools/">Tools</a></li>
<li id="menu-item-41" class="menu-item menu-item-type-custom menu-item-object-custom menu-item-41"><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/atoms/">ATOMs</a></li>
<li id="menu-item-119884" class="menu-item menu-item-type-custom menu-item-object-custom menu-item-119884"><a target="_blank" href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/unit42">Security Consulting</a></li>
<li id="menu-item-81229" class="menu-item menu-item-type-post_type menu-item-object-page menu-item-81229"><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/about-unit-42/">About Us</a></li>
<li id="menu-item-121229" class="menu-item menu-item-type-custom menu-item-object-custom menu-item-121229"><a href="https://start.paloaltonetworks.com/contact-unit42.html"><b style="color:#C84727">Under Attack?</b></a></li>
</ul>  </div>
</nav>
<div class="panClean pan-template-home" id="main-nav-menu-cont" style="display:none;">
    <div class="cleanHeader mainNavigationComp baseComponent parbase">
        <div class="productNav2021Component dark default" id="PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC">
        </div>
  </div>
<div class="cleanTopHtml htmlComp baseComponent parbase"><div class="base-component-spacer spacer-none  "></div>
</div>
</div>
<!--  Start: Scripts Migrated From Unit42-v5 -->
<script type="text/javascript">
	function getCookie(cname) {
	 	var name = cname + "=";
  		var decodedCookie = decodeURIComponent(document.cookie);
		var ca = decodedCookie.split(';');
  		for(var i = 0; i <ca.length; i++) {
    			var c = ca[i];
    			while (c.charAt(0) == ' ') {
     				 c = c.substring(1);
    			}
    			if (c.indexOf(name) == 0) {
    				 return c.substring(name.length, c.length);
    			}
  		}
  		return "";
	}

	var referer = "";//sessionStorage.container;
	var pcontainer = sessionStorage.getItem("container");
	var searchResultsPagePath = "";
	if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Prisma')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Prisma' ;
	}        
        else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Cortex')!=-1)){
            if( pcontainer.indexOf('CloudCortex') != -1){
                referer = 'CloudCortex' ;
            }
            else{
                referer = 'Cortex' ;
            }	    
	}
        else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Sase')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Sase' ;
	}
	else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Unit')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Unit' ;
	}
	else if(((pcontainer) && pcontainer.indexOf('Ngfw')!=-1)){
	    referer = 'Ngfw' ;
	}
        var fromRef = document.referrer;
        var nContainer = getCookie("navContainer");
        if(nContainer){//If user is coming from main site, we need to reset the container		
		if(fromRef  && fromRef.indexOf("prismacloud.io")!=-1){
                        referer = 'Prisma' ;
                        sessionStorage.setItem("container","Prisma");
                } else if(fromRef.indexOf("paloaltonetworks.com")!=-1 || fromRef.indexOf("paloaltonetworks.jp")!=-1 ){
                        if(nContainer.indexOf('Prisma') != -1){
                            referer = 'Prisma' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Prisma");
                        }
                        if(nContainer.indexOf('Cortex') != -1){
                            if( nContainer.indexOf('CloudCortex') != -1){
                                referer = 'CloudCortex';
                                sessionStorage.setItem("container","CloudCortex");
                            }
                            else{
                                referer = 'Cortex';
                                sessionStorage.setItem("container","Cortex");
                            }                            
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Sase') != -1){
                            referer = 'Sase' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Sase");
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Unit') != -1){
                            referer = 'Unit' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Unit");
                        }
			if(nContainer.indexOf('Ngfw') != -1){
                            referer = 'Ngfw' ;
                            sessionStorage.setItem("container","Ngfw");
                        }
			document.cookie = 'navContainer=; path=/; domain=.paloaltonetworks.com; expires=' + new Date(0).toUTCString();
		}
	}
if(referer != "Prisma" && referer != "CloudCortex" && referer != "Cortex" && referer != "Sase" && referer != "Unit" && referer != "Ngfw") {
    referer = 'Unit' ;
    sessionStorage.setItem("container","Unit");
}
function callMainSitePrismaNavHTML(){
    
    var referrer_domain = 'https://www.paloaltonetworks.com';
    sessionStorage.setItem("domain",referrer_domain);
    if(referer == 'Prisma'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderPrisma.prismaRenderer.html';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=prisma&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/prismasearch";
        }
    if(referer == 'CloudCortex'){
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=cortexcloud&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/cortexcloudsearch";	
    }
    if(referer == 'Cortex'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderCortex.cortexRenderer.html';	
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=cortex&domain=en';	
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/cortexsearch";	
    }
    if(referer == 'Sase'){
        //var menu_url = referrer_domain+'/_jcr_content/globals/cleanHeaderSase.saseRenderer.html';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=sase&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/sasesearch";
    }
    if(referer == 'Unit'){
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=unit42&domain=en';
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/content/pan/en_US/search/unit42search";
		    }
    if(referer == 'Ngfw'){
        //var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/ngfw-cdss-nav-renderer.php';
        var menu_url = 'https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/unit-nav-renderer.php?type=ngfw&domain=en'; 
        searchResultsPagePath = referrer_domain+"/search/ngfwcdsssearch";
    }
    httpGet(menu_url,'menu_html');
    document.getElementById('main-nav-menu-cont').removeAttribute("style");
}
function addStyle(styles) {            
    /* Create style document */
    var css = document.createElement('style');
    css.type = 'text/css';

    if (css.styleSheet) 
        css.styleSheet.cssText = styles;
    else 
        css.appendChild(document.createTextNode(styles));

    /* Append style to the tag name */
    document.getElementsByTagName("head")[0].appendChild(css);
}
function httpGet(theUrl,req_type) {
    if (window.XMLHttpRequest) { // code for IE7+, Firefox, Chrome, Opera, Safari
        xmlhttp=new XMLHttpRequest();
    } else {// code for IE6, IE5
        xmlhttp=new ActiveXObject("Microsoft.XMLHTTP");
    }
    xmlhttp.onreadystatechange=function()
    {
        if (xmlhttp.readyState==4 && xmlhttp.status==200)
        {
            if(req_type == 'menu_html'){
            var nav_text = xmlhttp.responseText.replaceAll('https://static.cloud.coveo.com/searchui/v2.9159/js/CoveoJsSearch.Lazy.min.js', '');
            nav_text = nav_text.replaceAll('src="/', 'src="'+maindomain_lang+'/');
            nav_text = nav_text.replaceAll("'/content", "'"+maindomain_lang+"/content");
                        
            document.getElementById("PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC").innerHTML = nav_text.replaceAll('href="/', 'href="'+maindomain_lang+'/');

            var lozad_back = document.getElementsByClassName('lozad-background');
            Array.prototype.forEach.call(lozad_back, function(el) {
            // Do stuff here
            var el_back_img_path = el.getAttribute('data-background-image');
            var first_pos = el_back_img_path.indexOf("'");
            var last_pos = el_back_img_path.indexOf("'",first_pos+1);
            el_back_img_path = el_back_img_path.substring(first_pos+1,last_pos);
            el.setAttribute("data-background-image",main_site_url+el_back_img_path);
            });
            const observer_lozad = lozad('.lozad, .lozad-background'); // lazy loads elements with default selector as '.lozad'
            observer_lozad.observe();
            }
            if(req_type == 'head_inline_css'){
            addStyle(xmlhttp.responseText);
            }
        }
    }
    xmlhttp.open("GET", theUrl, true );
    xmlhttp.send();    
}    
    
if(referer == 'Prisma' || referer == 'CloudCortex' || referer == 'Cortex' || referer == 'Sase' || referer == 'Unit' || referer == 'Ngfw'){
    const article = document.querySelector('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC');
    if(referer == 'Prisma'){
        article.dataset.type = 'prisma';
        $('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC').removeClass('default').addClass('defaultRedesigned');
    }
    else if(referer == 'CloudCortex'){
        article.dataset.type = 'cloudcortex';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Cortex'){
        article.dataset.type = 'cortex';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Sase'){
        article.dataset.type = 'sase';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Unit'){
        article.dataset.type = 'unit';
    }
    else if(referer == 'Ngfw'){
        article.dataset.type = 'ngfw';
    }
    //set class to default
    if(referer == 'Unit' || referer == 'Ngfw'){
        $('#PAN_2021_NAV_ASYNC').removeClass('default').addClass('defaultRedesigned');
    }
    callMainSitePrismaNavHTML();
}
</script>
<!--  End: Scripts Migrated From Unit42-v5 -->

      </section> 
    <section class="section blog-contents"> 
      <div class="pa blog-editor">
        <div class="l-container">
          <div class="be__wrapper">
            <div class="be__contents">
              <div class="be__contents-wrapper">
                <h2><a id="post-170663-_4lt92rr5muov"></a>Executive Summary</h2>
<p>We discovered an aspect of Azure’s <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-overview" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Private Endpoint</a> architecture that could expose Azure resources to denial of service (DoS) attacks. In this article, we explore how both intentional and inadvertent acts could result in limited access to Azure resources through the Azure Private Link mechanism. We uncovered this issue while investigating irregular behavior in Azure test environments.</p>
<p>The risk is present in three scenarios:</p>
<ul>
<li>Accidental - internal: A network administrator deploys Private Endpoints to improve network security within an Azure environment.</li>
<li>Accidental - vendor: A third-party vendor deploys Private Endpoints as part of its solution, for example to enable resource scanning by a security product.</li>
<li>Malicious - attacker: A threat actor who gained access to an Azure environment intentionally deploys Private Endpoints as part of a DoS attack.</li>
</ul>
<p>Our research indicates that over 5% of Azure storage accounts currently operate with configurations that are subject to this DoS issue. In most environments, at least one resource in each of the following services is susceptible:</p>
<ul>
<li>Key Vault</li>
<li>CosmosDB</li>
<li>Azure Container Registry (ACR)</li>
<li>Function Apps</li>
<li>OpenAI accounts</li>
</ul>
<p>This issue has the potential to affect organizations in multiple ways. For example, denying service to storage accounts could cause <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/azure-functions/functions-overview" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Azure Functions within FunctionApps</a> and subsequent updates to these apps to fail. In another scenario, the risk could lead to DoS to Key Vaults, resulting in a ripple effect on processes that depend on secrets within the vault.</p>
<p>Microsoft provides <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/dns/private-dns-fallback" target="_blank" rel="noopener">fallback to internet</a> advice that partially addresses this and other <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-private-endpoints?toc=%2Fazure%2Fstorage%2Fblobs%2Ftoc.json&amp;bc=%2Fazure%2Fstorage%2Fblobs%2Fbreadcrumb%2Ftoc.json#storage-access-constraints-for-clients-in-virtual-networks-with-private-endpoints" target="_blank" rel="noopener">known issues</a> associated with Private Endpoints.</p>
<p>We discuss these issues, provide potential solutions and suggest ways that defenders can scan environments for resources that are susceptible to DoS attacks.</p>
<p>Palo Alto Networks customers are better protected from the threats discussed in this article through the following products:</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cortex/cloud" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex Cloud</a></li>
</ul>
<p><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/unit42/assess/cloud-security-assessment" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42 Cloud Security Assessment</a> is a strategic evaluation service that reviews your organization's cloud infrastructure to identify misconfigurations and security gaps, enabling teams to strengthen their posture against cloud-based threats.</p>
<p>If you think you might have been compromised or have an urgent matter, contact the <a href="https://start.paloaltonetworks.com/contact-unit42.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42 Incident Response team</a>.</p>
<table style="width: 97.0182%;">
<thead>
<tr>
<td style="width: 35%;"><b>Related Unit 42 Topics</b></td>
<td style="width: 174.878%;"><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/microsoft-azure/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><b>Microsoft Azure</b></a><b>, </b><a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/category/cloud-cybersecurity-research/" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><b>Cloud Research</b></a></td>
</tr>
</thead>
</table>
<h2><a id="post-170663-_hpm7dtty3kcz"></a>Azure Private Link Key Components, Concepts and Flows</h2>
<p>As part of Azure’s networking offering, Microsoft created <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-link-overview" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Azure Private Link</a>. This mechanism offers a private, secure way to connect to supported Azure resources and Azure-hosted custom services using Azure’s backbone network.</p>
<h3><a id="post-170663-_qcbrxs43l1ih"></a>Definitions</h3>
<p>To understand the solution and the secure connection process of resources using it, let’s first define the key components and concepts.</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Service</strong>: Destination to which a connection is made</li>
<li><strong>Private Link</strong>: Azure implementation that allows and handles the connections</li>
<li><strong>Private Endpoint</strong>: A network interface within a customer’s virtual network that allows connectivity to the service</li>
<li><strong>Private DNS zone</strong>: The default Domain Name System (DNS) service that is used with Private Endpoints</li>
<li><strong>Virtual network link</strong>: The link created by default between a Private DNS zone and a virtual network</li>
<li><strong>DNS A record</strong>: A DNS record that maps a domain or hostname to an IP address</li>
<li><strong>Network ACL</strong>: Network access control lists (ACLs) define rules that allow or restrict traffic to a service, separate from the Private Link solution</li>
</ul>
<p>Azure resources expose public endpoints by default. These endpoints resolve through standard DNS infrastructure — either public Azure DNS, or customer-managed DNS resolvers. When a client queries a service name such as <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">mystorageaccount.blob.core.windows[.]net</span>, the DNS resolver returns a public IP address owned by Microsoft. The IP address allows connectivity over the public internet or Azure service endpoints, depending on the network access controls that are applied.</p>
<p>When Azure Private Link is introduced, DNS resolution behavior changes. A Private DNS zone — for example, <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">privatelink.blob.core.windows[.]net</span> — can be linked to one or more virtual networks. If a virtual network has a link to a Private DNS zone for a given service type, Azure’s DNS resolution logic prioritizes that zone when resolving matching service names. If a matching record exists, the name resolves to the private IP address of the Private Endpoint, instead of the public endpoint.</p>
<p>Organizations commonly deploy one of the following architectures:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Public-only architecture: </strong>Resources are accessed using their public endpoints and standard DNS resolution. Network ACLs, firewalls or service endpoints restrict access.</li>
<li><strong>Private-only architecture: </strong>All access to the resource occurs through Private Endpoints. Public network access is disabled, and DNS resolution is fully controlled through Private DNS zones.</li>
<li><strong>Hybrid architecture (public and private):</strong> Some virtual networks or workloads access the resource through Private Endpoints, while others continue using the public endpoint. This model is frequently used during migrations, phased rollouts, third-party integrations, or shared service environments.</li>
</ul>
<p>We use the example of storage accounts to demonstrate how Private Endpoints are used in Azure networking. However, the same concepts apply to any Private Link-supported service. By default, when a network administrator or a user with appropriate Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) permissions creates a Private Endpoint linked to a resource, a Private DNS zone is created with a virtual network link to the same virtual network as the Private Endpoint.</p>
<p>The DNS zone’s name has a predetermined structure that is based on the <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-dns" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Private Endpoint’s destination service</a> (e.g., <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">privatelink.blob.core.windows[.]net</span> for blob storage). Additionally, an A record is created in the DNS zone, linking the name of the destination resource and the IP address of the Private Endpoint.</p>
<p>There are two ways to connect between a virtual machine VM and a storage account with a Network ACL:</p>
<ul>
<li>Without a Private Endpoint (using public or service endpoints)</li>
<li>With a Private Endpoint</li>
</ul>
<h3><a id="post-170663-_s65n862fv1c4"></a>Connection Flows</h3>
<p>Figure 1 shows how a connection is made to a resource that does not use the Private Link solution.</p>
<figure id="attachment_170708" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-170708" style="width: 761px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-170708 size-full lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-3.png" alt="Diagram showing network architecture with four main components: VNET1 containing VM1, connected to a DNS Resolver and a Storage Account with a Network ACL listing two rules: 1. Allow VNET1, 2. Deny *. The connections are numbered to indicate the flow of interaction." width="761" height="416" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-170708" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 1. Connection flow without the Private Link solution.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The flow in this case is as follows:</p>
<ol>
<li>When trying to access the storage account, the VM attempts to resolve the account’s name to an IP address. This usually occurs through an external DNS resolver, and the traffic traverses the public internet.</li>
<li>If the resolver has a record for the storage account, it returns the corresponding IP address.</li>
<li>Once the address is obtained, the VM tries to connect to the storage account.</li>
<li>The storage account then evaluates the virtual machine’s IP address against its Network ACL. If the connection is allowed, the storage account responds with the requested information.</li>
</ol>
<p>Figure 2 shows how the same connection is made when a Private Endpoint is used.</p>
<figure id="attachment_170719" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-170719" style="width: 808px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-170719 size-full lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/2-2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-4.png" alt="Diagram showing network communication within Azure. VNET1 connects to a VM1 and a Private DNS Zone labeled as &quot;privatelink.blob.core.windows.net.&quot; A Private Endpoint interacts between VM1 and a Storage Account, illustrating the flow of data marked with numbers 1 to 6, indicating the sequence of communication." width="808" height="411" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/2-2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-4.png 808w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/2-2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-4-786x400.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/2-2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-4-768x391.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 808px) 100vw, 808px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-170719" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 2. Connection flow with the Private Link solution.</figcaption></figure>
<ol>
<li>Initially, the VM attempts to resolve the account’s name to an IP address. If a virtual network link exists in the virtual network (VNET) to a Private DNS zone that points to a Private Link service, Azure’s Private Link mechanism forces resolution using the Private DNS zone. This occurs when the connection is to a Private Link service of the same type (e.g., blob storage).</li>
<li>When the DNS resolver identifies a matching A record, it provides the VM with the corresponding IP address.</li>
<li>The VM then connects to the IP address, which belongs to the Private Endpoint.</li>
<li>The Private Endpoint acts as a network interface for the storage account, evaluating the traffic and then forwarding it to the storage account.</li>
<li>The storage account evaluates the request and supplies a response through the Private Link solution.</li>
<li>The response is presented to the VM, which essentially accesses the storage account using Azure’s backbone network.</li>
</ol>
<h2><a id="post-170663-_wven14kmgum2"></a>Potential Private Link DNS Dangers</h2>
<p>As outlined above, interactions with a Private Link service from a virtual network configured with a Private DNS zone for that service type are forced to resolve through the Private DNS zone.</p>
<p>Consider an environment where VM1 in VNET1 successfully accesses a storage account using its public endpoint. DNS resolution occurs through the default public DNS infrastructure, and access is permitted by the storage account’s Network ACLs. At this stage, the workload is operating normally.</p>
<p>Later, a Private Endpoint is created for the storage account in VNET2 — either intentionally, accidentally, or by a third-party deployment. As part of this process, a Private DNS zone for blob storage (<span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">privatelink.blob.core.windows[.]net</span>) is linked to VNET1 or shared across virtual networks.</p>
<p>Once this DNS zone is linked, Azure’s DNS resolution logic forces all blob storage name resolution in VNET1 through the Private DNS zone. However, because no “A” record exists in the zone for the storage account within the context of VNET1, DNS resolution fails. VM1 can no longer resolve the storage account hostname and is unable to connect — even though the public endpoint remains accessible and unchanged.</p>
<p>This configuration change effectively creates a denial-of-service condition for workloads in VNET1 that previously functioned correctly. The outage is triggered solely by a DNS resolution side effect introduced by the Private Link configuration, without any change to the target resource itself.</p>
<p>Figure 3 demonstrates this example.</p>
<figure id="attachment_170730" aria-describedby="caption-attachment-170730" style="width: 939px" class="wp-caption alignnone"><img  class="wp-image-170730 size-full lozad"  data-src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/3-2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-5.png" alt="Diagram showing two virtual networks, VNET1 and VNET2, connected by a storage account. VNET1 includes a VM1 and a private DNS zone. The storage account is positioned between both networks. This setup may result in failed connections, as indicated by red crosses on paths from VM1 to the storage account. VNET2 contains a private DNS zone and a linked private endpoint." width="939" height="427" data-srcset="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/3-2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-5.png 939w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/3-2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-5-786x357.png 786w, https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/3-2641_Azure-Private-Endpoints-Figures-5-768x349.png 768w" sizes="(max-width: 939px) 100vw, 939px" /><figcaption id="caption-attachment-170730" class="wp-caption-text">Figure 3. Potential issue caused by using the Private Link solution.</figcaption></figure>
<p>The figure above shows that VM1 attempts to connect to the storage account. This storage account has a Private Endpoint in VNET2 and does not have a Private Endpoint in VNET1. Hypothetically, VM1 could try to connect using the storage account’s public endpoint. This would work if the VM’s virtual network did not have a Private DNS zone resolving to the same resource type. In this case, however, the Private DNS zone and the service are Private Link registered, so Private Link forces resolution through the Private DNS zone.</p>
<p>The process shown in Figure 3 occurs as follows:</p>
<ol>
<li>Private Link recognizes the blob storage Private DNS zone in VNET1 and identifies that the storage account is Private Link registered. Private Link forces DNS resolution through the Private DNS zone.</li>
<li>The configuration in Figure 3 shows that no record was created for the storage account in the Private DNS zone linked to VNET1. As such, the virtual machine cannot resolve the service.</li>
<li>Due to the DNS resolution issue in step 2, the subsequent steps – in which VM1 sends a request to the storage account and receives a response from it – do not occur.</li>
</ol>
<p>This scenario causes a partial denial of service: any resource in VNET1 that tries to access the storage account will not be able to.</p>
<p>This risk can also be present when using local DNS resolvers, depending on the configurations and the records added. Additionally, although our example relates specifically to blob storage, any service that supports <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/availability" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Azure Private Link</a> is potentially at risk.</p>
<h2><a id="post-170663-_5itnnw4y71bw"></a>Mitigations and Recommendations</h2>
<p>Azure Private Link was originally intended as a binary solution, to be either fully enabled, or disabled. When the service is implemented as intended, users can only access resources that use Private Link through those resources' Private Endpoints. This approach eliminates the need to use a combination of private, public and service endpoints. However, as over 5% of Azure storage accounts are configured in a way that is subject to this issue, we recognize the need to provide solutions that address the risks introduced by these implementations.</p>
<h3><a id="post-170663-_i7zza0t7ldco"></a>Mitigations</h3>
<p>Microsoft acknowledges that the binary nature of the solution is a known issue and mentions it in <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-private-endpoints?toc=%2Fazure%2Fstorage%2Fblobs%2Ftoc.json&amp;bc=%2Fazure%2Fstorage%2Fblobs%2Fbreadcrumb%2Ftoc.json#storage-access-constraints-for-clients-in-virtual-networks-with-private-endpoints" target="_blank" rel="noopener">their documentation</a>. Microsoft also provides a partial solution: enabling a <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/dns/private-dns-fallback" target="_blank" rel="noopener">fallback to internet</a> option when creating a virtual network link. Using this fallback solution, when the DNS Resolver cannot find a record that matches the requested service, it falls back to the public internet. While this solution solves the issue, it does not necessarily match the main concept of Azure Private Link, which is to traverse Azure’s backbone network rather than the public internet.</p>
<p>A second, partial solution is to manually add a record for the affected resource in the necessary Private DNS zone. This option is less appropriate for large production environments, as it creates additional operational overhead.</p>
<p>Although neither fallback nor manually adding a record is a definitive solution, combining the above approaches with comprehensive discovery will help with mapping, finding and remediating affected configurations.</p>
<h3><a id="post-170663-_hq8r4m5m1vm7"></a>Comprehensive Discovery and Scanning</h3>
<p>There are two ways to scan and identify resources that are potentially at risk:</p>
<ol>
<li>Scanning for resources with each service individually</li>
<li>Using <a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/governance/resource-graph/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Azure Resource Graph Explorer</a></li>
</ol>
<p>The second scanning method is more efficient and can easily be modified to match most resource types. We created a graph query that retrieves a list of all the virtual networks in the environment that are linked to a blob storage Private DNS zone (<span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">privatelink.blob.core.windows[.]net</span>). These virtual networks are forced to resolve blob storage resources through their Private Endpoints when communicating with Private Link registered resources.</p><!-- Crayon Syntax Highlighter v_2.7.2_beta -->

		<div id="crayon-69716961610e5517433239" class="crayon-syntax crayon-theme-classic crayon-font-monaco crayon-os-pc print-yes notranslate" data-settings=" minimize scroll-mouseover wrap" style=" margin-top: 12px; margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 12px !important; line-height: 15px !important;">
		
			<div class="crayon-toolbar" data-settings=" mouseover overlay hide delay" style="font-size: 12px !important;height: 18px !important; line-height: 18px !important;"><span class="crayon-title"></span>
			<div class="crayon-tools" style="font-size: 12px !important;height: 18px !important; line-height: 18px !important;"><div class="crayon-button crayon-nums-button" title="Toggle Line Numbers"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-plain-button" title="Toggle Plain Code"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-wrap-button" title="Toggle Line Wrap"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-expand-button" title="Expand Code"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-copy-button" title="Copy"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-popup-button" title="Open Code In New Window"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div></div></div>
			<div class="crayon-info" style="min-height: 16.8px !important; line-height: 16.8px !important;"></div>
			<div class="crayon-plain-wrap"><textarea  class="crayon-plain print-no" data-settings="dblclick" readonly style="-moz-tab-size:4; -o-tab-size:4; -webkit-tab-size:4; tab-size:4; font-size: 12px !important; line-height: 15px !important;">
resources
| where type == "microsoft.network/privatednszones/virtualnetworklinks"
| extend 
    zone = tostring(split(id, "/virtualNetworkLinks")[0]),
    vnetId = tostring(properties.virtualNetwork.id)
| join kind=inner (
    resources
    | where type == "microsoft.network/privatednszones"
    | where name == "privatelink.blob.core.windows.net"
    | project zoneId = id
) on $left.zone == $right.zoneId
| project vnetId</textarea></div>
			<div class="crayon-main" style="">
				<table class="crayon-table">

				</td>
						<td class="crayon-code"><div class="crayon-pre" style="font-size: 12px !important; line-height: 15px !important; -moz-tab-size:4; -o-tab-size:4; -webkit-tab-size:4; tab-size:4;"><div class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">extend </span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610e5517433239-4"><span class="crayon-i">vnetId</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">tostring</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-i">properties</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">virtualNetwork</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">id</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610e5517433239-6"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span id="crayon-69716961610e5517433239-8"><span class="crayon-h">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</span><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">where </span><span class="crayon-i">type</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>==<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-s">"microsoft.network/privatednszones"</span></div><div class="crayon-line" id="crayon-69716961610e5517433239-9"><span class="crayon-h">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</span><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">where </span><span class="crayon-e">project </span><span class="crayon-i">zoneId</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">id</span></div><div class="crayon-line" class="crayon-sy">$</span><span class="crayon-i">right</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">zoneId</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610e5517433239-12"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">project </span><span class="crayon-i">vnetId</span></div></div></td>
					</tr>
				</table>
			</div>
		</div>
<!-- [Format Time: 0.0002 seconds] -->
<p>In addition, it is important to identify which virtual networks interact with blob storage resources that do not have Private Endpoint connections. We created the following query for this purpose. This query retrieves storage accounts that allow access to their public endpoint and do not have a Private Endpoint connection.</p><!-- Crayon Syntax Highlighter v_2.7.2_beta -->

		<div id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696" class="crayon-syntax crayon-theme-classic crayon-font-monaco crayon-os-pc print-yes notranslate" data-settings=" minimize scroll-mouseover wrap" style=" margin-top: 12px; margin-bottom: 12px; font-size: 12px !important; line-height: 15px !important;">
		
			<div class="crayon-toolbar" data-settings=" mouseover overlay hide delay" style="font-size: 12px !important;height: 18px !important; line-height: 18px !important;"><span class="crayon-title"></span>
			<div class="crayon-tools" style="font-size: 12px !important;height: 18px !important; line-height: 18px !important;"><div class="crayon-button crayon-nums-button" title="Toggle Line Numbers"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-plain-button" title="Toggle Plain Code"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-wrap-button" title="Toggle Line Wrap"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-expand-button" title="Expand Code"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-copy-button" title="Copy"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div><div class="crayon-button crayon-popup-button" title="Open Code In New Window"><div class="crayon-button-icon"></div></div></div></div>
			<div class="crayon-info" style="min-height: 16.8px !important; line-height: 16.8px !important;"></div>
			<div class="crayon-plain-wrap"><textarea  class="crayon-plain print-no" data-settings="dblclick" readonly style="-moz-tab-size:4; -o-tab-size:4; -webkit-tab-size:4; tab-size:4; font-size: 12px !important; line-height: 15px !important;">
Resources
| where type == "microsoft.storage/storageaccounts"
| extend publicNetworkAccess = properties.publicNetworkAccess
| extend defaultAction = properties.networkAcls.defaultAction
| extend vnetRules = properties.networkAcls.virtualNetworkRules
| extend ipRules = properties.networkAcls.ipRules
| extend privateEndpoints = properties.privateEndpointConnections
| where publicNetworkAccess == "Enabled"
| where defaultAction == "Deny"
| where (isnull(privateEndpoints) or array_length(privateEndpoints) == 0)
| extend allowedVnets = iif(isnull(vnetRules), 0, array_length(vnetRules))
| extend allowedIps = iif(isnull(ipRules), 0, array_length(ipRules))
| where allowedVnets &gt; 0 or allowedIps &gt; 0
| project id, name, vnetRules, ipRules</textarea></div>
			<div class="crayon-main" style="">
				<table class="crayon-table">
					<tr class="crayon-row">
				<td class="crayon-nums " data-settings="show">
					<div class="crayon-nums-content" style="font-size: 12px !important; line-height: 15px !important;"><div class="crayon-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-1">1</div><div class="crayon-num crayon-striped-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-2">2</div><div class="crayon-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-3">3</div><div class="crayon-num crayon-striped-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-4">4</div><div class="crayon-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-5">5</div><div class="crayon-num crayon-striped-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-6">6</div><div class="crayon-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-7">7</div><div class="crayon-num crayon-striped-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-8">8</div><div class="crayon-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-9">9</div><div class="crayon-num crayon-striped-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-10">10</div><div class="crayon-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-11">11</div><div class="crayon-num crayon-striped-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-12">12</div><div class="crayon-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-13">13</div><div class="crayon-num crayon-striped-num" data-line="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-14">14</div></div>
				</td>
						<td class="crayon-code"><div class="crayon-pre" style="font-size: 12px !important; line-height: 15px !important; -moz-tab-size:4; -o-tab-size:4; -webkit-tab-size:4; tab-size:4;"><div class="crayon-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-1"><span class="crayon-i">Resources</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-2"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">where </span><span class="crayon-i">type</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>==<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-s">"microsoft.storage/storageaccounts"</span></div><div class="crayon-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-3"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">extend </span><span class="crayon-i">publicNetworkAccess</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">properties</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">publicNetworkAccess</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-4"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">extend </span><span class="crayon-i">defaultAction</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">properties</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">networkAcls</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">defaultAction</span></div><div class="crayon-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-5"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">extend </span><span class="crayon-i">vnetRules</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">properties</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">networkAcls</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">virtualNetworkRules</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-6"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">extend </span><span class="crayon-i">ipRules</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">properties</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">networkAcls</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">ipRules</span></div><div class="crayon-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-7"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">extend </span><span class="crayon-i">privateEndpoints</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">properties</span><span class="crayon-sy">.</span><span class="crayon-i">privateEndpointConnections</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-8"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">where </span><span class="crayon-i">publicNetworkAccess</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>==<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-s">"Enabled"</span></div><div class="crayon-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-9"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">where </span><span class="crayon-i">defaultAction</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>==<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-s">"Deny"</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-10"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">where</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-e">isnull</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-i">privateEndpoints</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-st">or</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">array_length</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-i">privateEndpoints</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>==<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-cn">0</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span></div><div class="crayon-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-11"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">extend </span><span class="crayon-i">allowedVnets</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">iif</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-e">isnull</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-i">vnetRules</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span><span class="crayon-sy">,</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-cn">0</span><span class="crayon-sy">,</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">array_length</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-i">vnetRules</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-12"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">extend </span><span class="crayon-i">allowedIps</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span>=<span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">iif</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-e">isnull</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-i">ipRules</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span><span class="crayon-sy">,</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-cn">0</span><span class="crayon-sy">,</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">array_length</span><span class="crayon-sy">(</span><span class="crayon-i">ipRules</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span><span class="crayon-sy">)</span></div><div class="crayon-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-13"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">where </span><span class="crayon-i">allowedVnets</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-h">&gt;</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-cn">0</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-st">or</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">allowedIps</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-h">&gt;</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-cn">0</span></div><div class="crayon-line crayon-striped-line" id="crayon-69716961610f0112875696-14"><span class="crayon-sy">|</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-e">project </span><span class="crayon-i">id</span><span class="crayon-sy">,</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">name</span><span class="crayon-sy">,</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">vnetRules</span><span class="crayon-sy">,</span><span class="crayon-h"> </span><span class="crayon-i">ipRules</span></div></div></td>
					</tr>
				</table>
			</div>
		</div>
<!-- [Format Time: 0.0003 seconds] -->
<p>This query identifies allowed virtual networks and also retrieves resources that allow specific IP addresses. This is useful when there is limited visibility into the DNS configurations for virtual networks, making it difficult to determine whether or not the configurations are affected.</p>
<p>Defenders should be aware that even resources that do not have Network ACLs could still be at risk. However, there is no way to use the configurations to determine which virtual networks, if any, attempt to communicate with such resources. To address this risk, use network logs to identify connections made from Azure network ranges to susceptible resources.</p>
<p>Defenders can change and match the resource and the Private DNS zone details in the above <span style="font-family: 'courier new', courier, monospace;">Resources</span> query to detect Private Link-supported resources that are at risk.</p>
<h2><a id="post-170663-_2an8ryq91inv"></a>Conclusion</h2>
<p>Fully understanding the limitations of Azure Private Link is vital to securing networks that rely on it. With certain configurations of components and architecture, the binary nature of Azure Private Link can lead to connectivity loss and in some cases, could enable DoS attacks.</p>
<p>Two of the possible solutions to secure Private Endpoints against this risk include:</p>
<ul>
<li>Enabling fallback to public DNS resolution</li>
<li>Manually adding DNS records for affected resources</li>
</ul>
<p>Defenders can increase the effectiveness of these solutions by querying resources, conducting comprehensive network scanning to identify resources at risk, and implementing the necessary protections.</p>
<h3><a id="post-170663-_lsbythhc3nz9"></a>Palo Alto Networks Protection and Mitigation</h3>
<p>Palo Alto Networks customers are better protected from the threats discussed in this article through the following products:</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/cortex/cloud" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cortex Cloud</a> customers are better protected from the misuse of the Azure Private Endpoints discussed within this article through the proper placement of Cortex Cloud <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/r/Cortex-CLOUD/Cortex-Cloud-Runtime-Security-Documentation/Endpoint-protection" target="_blank" rel="noopener">XDR endpoint agent</a> and <a href="https://docs-cortex.paloaltonetworks.com/r/Cortex-CLOUD/Cortex-Cloud-Runtime-Security-Documentation/Serverless-function-posture-security" target="_blank" rel="noopener">serverless agents</a> within a cloud environment. Cortex Cloud is designed to protect a cloud’s posture and runtime operations against these types of threats. It helps detect and prevent the malicious operations or configuration alterations or exploitation discussed within this article.</li>
</ul>
<p><a href="https://www.paloaltonetworks.com/unit42/assess/cloud-security-assessment" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42 Cloud Security Assessment</a> is a strategic evaluation service that reviews your organization's cloud infrastructure to identify misconfigurations and security gaps, enabling teams to strengthen their posture against cloud-based threats.</p>
<p>If you think you might have been compromised or have an urgent matter, get in touch with the <a href="https://start.paloaltonetworks.com/contact-unit42.html" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Unit 42 Incident Response team</a> or call:</p>
<ul>
<li>North America: Toll Free: +1 (866) 486-4842 (866.4.UNIT42)</li>
<li>UK: +44.20.3743.3660</li>
<li>Europe and Middle East: +31.20.299.3130</li>
<li>Asia: +65.6983.8730</li>
<li>Japan: +81.50.1790.0200</li>
<li>Australia: +61.2.4062.7950</li>
<li>India: 000 800 050 45107</li>
</ul>
<p>Palo Alto Networks has shared these findings with our fellow Cyber Threat Alliance (CTA) members. CTA members use this intelligence to rapidly deploy protections to their customers and to systematically disrupt malicious cyber actors. Learn more about the <a href="https://www.cyberthreatalliance.org">Cyber Threat Alliance</a>.</p>
<h2>Additional Resources</h2>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/private-endpoint-dns" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Azure Private Endpoint private DNS zone values</a> – Microsoft documentation</li>
<li><a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/private-link/availability" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Azure Private Link availability</a> – Microsoft documentation</li>
<li><a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/governance/resource-graph/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Azure Resource Graph Explorer</a> – Microsoft documentation</li>
<li><a href="https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-private-endpoints?toc=%2Fazure%2Fstorage%2Fblobs%2Ftoc.json&amp;bc=%2Fazure%2Fstorage%2Fblobs%2Fbreadcrumb%2Ftoc.json#storage-access-constraints-for-clients-in-virtual-networks-with-private-endpoints" target="_blank" rel="noopener">​​Storage access constraints for clients in virtual networks with private endpoints</a> – Microsoft documentation</li>
</ul>

                    <h4 class="post-title">Cloud Discovery With AzureHound</h4>
                  </a>
                  <ul class="card-tags" role="list">
				<li role="listitem">
					<a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/control-plane/" title="control plane" role="link" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="dos-attacks-and-azure-private-endpoint:related-resources:Cloud Discovery With AzureHound:control plane">Control plane</a>

				<li role="listitem">
					<a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/curious-serpens/" title="Curious Serpens" role="link" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="dos-attacks-and-azure-private-endpoint:related-resources:Cloud Discovery With AzureHound:Curious Serpens">Curious Serpens</a>
				</li>
				<li role="listitem">
					<a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/data-plane/" title="data plane" role="link" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="dos-attacks-and-azure-private-endpoint:related-resources:Cloud Discovery With AzureHound:data plane">Data plane</a>
				</li></ul>                </div>
                <div class="card-content__link">
                                    <a class="hyperlink" href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/threat-actor-misuse-of-azurehound/" title="Cloud Discovery With AzureHound" role="link" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="dos-attacks-and-azure-private-endpoint:related-resources:Cloud Discovery With AzureHound:read now">
                    Read now                    <img src="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/wp-content/themes/unit42-v6/dist/images/icons/icon-right-arrow-withtail.svg" alt="Right arrow">
                  </a>
                </div>
              </div>
            </div>

              <div class="card-media " >

              <div class="card-content">

                  </a>
                  <ul class="card-tags" role="list">
				<li role="listitem">
					<a href="https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/tag/linux-malware/" title="Linux Malware" role="link" data-page-track="true" data-page-track-value="dos-attacks-and-azure-private-endpoint:related-resources:The Evolution of Linux Binaries in Targeted Cloud Operations:Linux Malware">Linux Malware</a>
